skill.name: routeros-command-tree
skill.description: RouterOS command tree introspection via /console/inspect API. Use when: building tools that parse RouterOS commands, generating API schemas from RouterOS, working with /console/inspect, mapping CLI commands to REST verbs, traversing the RouterOS command hierarchy, or when the user mentions inspect, command tree, RAML, or OpenAPI generation for RouterOS.
skill.summary: Explore the RouterOS CLI hierarchy and generate API schemas from the command tree.
skill.author: tikoci

security_audit.summary: All 138 static findings are false positives. The skill is a documentation file containing markdown with code examples. The static scanner incorrectly flagged markdown syntax (backticks for code formatting), documentation code snippets (fetch examples), relative markdown links, and misinterpreted documentation text as security issues. No executable code exists in this skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown Code Formatting Misidentified as Command Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: Static scanner flagged markdown backticks (code fences and inline code) as 'Ruby/shell backtick execution'. This is documentation-only with no executable code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documentation Code Examples Misidentified as Network Calls
dangerous_patterns.description: Static scanner flagged TypeScript fetch() examples as actual network calls. These are documentation examples showing API patterns, not live network requests.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown Links Misidentified as Path Traversal
dangerous_patterns.description: Static scanner flagged relative markdown links as path traversal. The '..' in line 326 is a valid markdown link to another documentation file in the repository.

content.user_title: Generate RouterOS API Schemas from Command Tree
content.value_statement: Building tools that interact with RouterOS requires understanding the command hierarchy. This skill provides the knowledge to programmatically explore the RouterOS command tree via the /console/inspect API and generate RAML or OpenAPI schemas.
actual_capabilities: Navigate the RouterOS CLI hierarchy (dir, path, cmd, arg nodes)
Query /console/inspect API for command syntax and descriptions
Walk the full command tree recursively with proper error handling
Map RouterOS CLI commands to REST API verbs (GET, PUT, PATCH, DELETE)
Generate OpenAPI 3.0 and RAML 1.0 schemas from the command tree
Parse inspect.json files offline without live router queries
limitations: Requires access to a RouterOS 7.x device or cached inspect.json file
Cannot execute commands or modify router configuration
Some argument paths (where, do, else, rule) crash the inspect API and must be skipped
Schema generation is version-specific to each RouterOS release

use_cases.title: Build API Documentation Tools
use_cases.description: Generate comprehensive API documentation from the live RouterOS command tree for your specific RouterOS version.
use_cases.target_user: API developers creating documentation for RouterOS integrations

use_cases.title: Create Code Generation Templates
use_cases.description: Use the command tree to generate typed client libraries or Terraform providers for RouterOS resources.
use_cases.target_user: SDK authors and infrastructure automation engineers

use_cases.title: Explore Available Commands
use_cases.description: Discover all available configuration options and their parameters for a specific RouterOS feature set.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Command Tree Exploration
prompt_templates.prompt: What are the available commands under /ip/firewall in RouterOS?
prompt_templates.scenario: When you need to discover what firewall configuration options exist

prompt_templates.title: API Endpoint Mapping
prompt_templates.prompt: Map the /interface/bridge commands to their corresponding REST API endpoints and HTTP verbs.
prompt_templates.scenario: When building an application that manages bridge configurations via API

prompt_templates.title: Argument Discovery
prompt_templates.prompt: What arguments does the /ip/route/add command accept? Show me the syntax for each.
prompt_templates.scenario: When writing code to add static routes programmatically

prompt_templates.title: Schema Generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate an OpenAPI 3.0 schema for the /queue/simple resource based on the RouterOS command tree.
prompt_templates.scenario: When creating API documentation or client SDKs for queue management

output_examples.input: What are the child nodes under /system/script?
output_examples.output:
The /system/script path contains these commands:
- run (cmd) - Execute a script
- add (cmd) - Create a new script
- remove (cmd) - Delete a script
- set (cmd) - Modify script properties
- print (cmd) - List scripts
- export (cmd) - Export script source

output_examples.input: How do I map the /ip/address CLI commands to REST API?
output_examples.output:
CLI to REST mapping:
- add → PUT /rest/ip/address (creates new entry)
- set → PATCH /rest/ip/address/{id} (updates existing)
- remove → DELETE /rest/ip/address/{id} (deletes entry)
- print → GET /rest/ip/address (lists all)
- get (single) → GET /rest/ip/address/{id}

best_practices: Use INSPECTFILE environment variable for testing to avoid repeated live router queries during development
Skip syntax lookups for arguments inside dangerous subtrees (where, do, else, rule, command, on-error) to prevent REST server crashes
Cache inspect.json per RouterOS version since the command tree varies significantly between releases
anti_patterns: Do not query syntax descriptions for every argument in a live router - this takes hours for full tree traversal
Do not ignore dangerous paths - calling fetchSyntax on arg nodes in those subtrees terminates the HTTP server process
Do not assume the tree is identical across RouterOS versions - always regenerate schemas for your target version

faq.question: What is /console/inspect in RouterOS?
faq.answer: It is a REST API endpoint that exposes the RouterOS command hierarchy. You can query it to list children of any path or get help text for commands and arguments.

faq.question: How do I avoid crashing the RouterOS REST server?
faq.answer: Skip syntax lookups for arguments inside subtrees containing: where, do, else, rule, command, on-error. These are scripting constructs that crash the inspect API when their arg nodes are queried.

faq.question: Can I work without a live RouterOS device?
faq.answer: Yes. Set the INSPECTFILE environment variable to point to a cached inspect.json file. Tools like rest2raml support this for offline schema generation.

faq.question: What is the difference between RAML and OpenAPI output?
faq.answer: Both represent the same command tree data. RAML 1.0 uses resource-oriented notation. OpenAPI 3.0 uses standard REST terminology with paths, methods, and request bodies.

faq.question: Why does PUT create entries instead of PUT updating?
faq.answer: RouterOS REST API uses PUT for create operations. PATCH is used for updates. This is the opposite of standard REST conventions where PUT is idempotent update and POST is create.

faq.question: How long does full tree traversal take?
faq.answer: Against a live router, full traversal takes many minutes due to thousands of sequential HTTP requests. With KVM-accelerated CHR it is faster but still time-consuming. Use cached inspect.json for development.
